senses.definition: to invite someone to enter a place, often a room, office, house, etc.

examples.x: We were asked in to join the celebration.

senses.definition: to put all one's attention into one thing

examples.x: It's important not to butt in when someone is sharing a personal story.
I didn't want to butt in, but I had relevant information to share.
I wish you wouldn't butt in every time I try to explain something.
He always butts in when we're discussing serious matters.

senses.definition: to add one's share of money, support, or guidance

examples.x: Family members chipped in to pay for the surprise party.
They always chip in when someone needs assistance.
Everyone chipped in for the farewell gift.
Everyone chipped in to buy a gift for their friend.
He chipped in by giving helpful feedback on the presentation.

senses.definition: to concentrate and direct all one's effort, attention, and focus toward achieving a particular goal

examples.x: Recognizing the importance of the project, she decided to dial in and focus on its successful completion.
The athlete knew it was time to dial in and give their best performance during the championship.
With the deadline approaching, the team needed to dial in and work collaboratively to meet their goals.
In preparation for the exam, he had to dial in and dedicate concentrated study time to grasp the material.
The entrepreneur had to dial in and navigate challenges strategically to ensure the success of the startup.

senses.definition: to visit a place or someone without a prior arrangement, often casually and briefly

examples.x: If you're in the area, feel free to drop in for a cup of tea.
We're having an open house this weekend, so you can drop in anytime.
She decided to drop in at the bookstore to browse for a new novel.
The neighbors often drop in for a chat and share news about the neighborhood.

senses.definition: to develop a positive relationship or connection with someone or a group, typically to gain their approval, favor, or influence

examples.x: He wanted to get in with the senior executives to advance his career.
She tried to get in with the cool kids at school to fit in.
Networking events provide opportunities to get in with industry professionals.
They hoped to get in with the influential members of the community.
Building a strong online presence can help you get in with potential clients.

senses.definition: to surrender to someone's demands, wishes, or desires, often after a period of resistance

examples.x: After a long negotiation, they finally gave in to the demands of the opposing party.
The child kept asking for a pet, and eventually, the parents gave in and got a puppy.
They initially disagreed on the vacation destination, but he eventually gave in to her choice.
He wanted to save money, but he gave in to the temptation and bought the expensive gadget.
Sarah tried to resist eating dessert, but the temptation was too strong, and she gave in to her cravings.
After hours of negotiation, she finally gave in to her friend and let her borrow the car.
Despite his determination to stick to his diet, Mark gave in to his friends and indulged in a slice of pizza.

senses.definition: to form a partnership or collaboration with someone or a group for a common purpose

examples.x: The artists are looking to go in with a gallery for their upcoming exhibition.
She's planning to go in with a group of friends to organize a charity event.
He decided to go in with a team of researchers to conduct the scientific experiment.
The two companies agreed to go in with each other to develop a new product.
Last year, they went in with a charity organization to build a school in a remote village.

senses.definition: to ask someone to come inside and join one at a particular place, typically one's home

examples.x: He offered to help carry her groceries and was invited in as a gesture of gratitude.
After a long journey, the weary travelers were invited in for a comfortable stay at a nearby inn.

senses.definition: to maintain a positive relationship or connection with someone, often for personal gain or advantage

examples.x: He always tries to keep in with the boss to secure promotions.
She wanted to keep in with her influential friends in the industry.
Some people keep in with their neighbors to have a supportive community.
Maintaining professionalism is essential to keep in with clients and partners.
He attempts to keep in with his in-laws to have a harmonious family life.

senses.definition: to make a brief stop or visit to someone or somewhere, particularly to check on them
to contribute to a task, usually alongside others

examples.x: When the community center needed repairs, everyone from the neighborhood pitched in.
The project was huge, but it became manageable when all team members pitched in.
If we all pitch in, we can finish cleaning the house in no time.
Each family member pitched in to cover the reunion's expenses.
The team pitched in to buy the coach a thank-you present at the end of the season.

senses.definition: to make a short, usually unplanned, visit to a place or person

examples.x: I'll pop in for a coffee later if you're free.
My neighbors often pop in to say hello.
If you're passing by the office, feel free to pop in and grab the book you left.
She popped in at her grandmother's house on her way home.
Whenever he's in town, he likes to pop in and check on his old friends.

senses.definition: to interrupt someone to say something

examples.x: He constantly puts in with jokes, even when the conversation is serious.
It's frustrating when people put in with irrelevant comments during important discussions.
Every time I try to speak, someone always puts in with their opinion.
During the discussion, Lisa felt compelled to put in and share her experience.
I was explaining the plan when Jane put in her thoughts.

senses.definition: to greet someone into a place

examples.x: The school principal saw in the new students during the orientation event.
The mayor will see in the special guests at the charity event.

senses.definition: to assist someone to become accustomed to a new environment
to guide someone to the designated room or space

examples.x: The usher showed in the audience and directed them to their seats.
The guide showed in the tour group, providing interesting facts along the way.

senses.definition: to act as a substitute for someone or something

examples.x: When the lead actor fell ill, a colleague had to stand in for the important scene.
The experienced employee offered to stand in for the new recruit during the training session.
A friend was kind enough to stand in for the babysitter when unexpected plans came up.
The lead actor fell ill, and they had to find someone to stand in at the last minute for the crucial scenes.
The keynote speaker couldn't make it to the conference, so they had to find someone to stand in and deliver the address.
The CEO had a scheduling conflict, so the vice president had to stand in and represent the company at the international summit.
